- The strategic insight is **relative tempo**: you don't need a perfect decision, you need a good-enough decision delivered faster than the opponent (or market) can adapt, so they fall behind and unravel.
- **Example — fighter combat (the origin):** Boyd showed the F-86 beat the faster MiG-15 in Korea because its bubble canopy and hydraulics let pilots transition between maneuvers — re-orient and re-act — quicker, even when out-performed on paper.
- **Example — startups vs. incumbents:** a lean team ships, watches real usage, re-orients, and re-ships weekly while a big competitor is still in a quarterly planning cycle — the startup is operating inside the incumbent's loop.
- **Example — incident response:** an on-call team observing live dashboards, orienting on a likely root cause, deciding a mitigation, and acting in minutes out-loops a slowly cascading outage.
author: Col. John Boyd (USAF)
when_to_use: When you face a fast-moving, adversarial, or fluid situation and tempo of decision-making is itself the advantage.
when_not_to_use: For one-time irreversible high-stakes commitments where careful single-shot analysis beats rapid iteration.
diagram_anchors: **OODA Loop** is Col. John Boyd's (USAF fighter pilot and strategist) decision cycle

name: RAPID Decision Making Framework
summary: Bain's role-assignment lens: names who Recommends, Agrees, Performs, Inputs, and Decides so a stalled or re-litigated call gets owned.
body_md:
- **RAPID**, devised by Bain & Company (Paul Rogers & Marcia Blenko in HBR's "Who Has the D?", 2006), assigns five distinct roles in a complex decision so exactly one person owns the call and the rest know their lane.
- **Recommend (R)** — drives the proposal: gathers data, consults stakeholders, weighs options, and makes the case. This is the engine of the decision.
- **Agree (A)** — holds a formal veto over the recommendation; must sign off before it proceeds. Reserve for genuine gatekeepers (legal, compliance, a partner) — not everyone with an opinion.
- **Perform (P)** — executes the decision once made and owns implementation. Naming them early surfaces feasibility concerns before the call is locked.
- **Input (I)** — consulted for facts and perspective to improve the recommendation, but advisory only — no veto. RAPID's key move is separating Input from Agree so "I disagree" never silently becomes a block.
- **Decide (D)** — the single accountable person who commits the call and answers for the outcome. One D, always — the whole framework exists to make this unambiguous.
- The letters are a loose mnemonic, **not a strict sequence**; in practice Input and Recommend iterate, then Agree, Decide, and Perform follow.
- **Example — pricing change:** the PM is R (builds the proposal), Sales and Support give I, Finance is A (veto if margins break), the VP Product is D, and RevOps is P (ships the new price in billing).
- **Example — Bain's classic case:** a global consumer-goods firm couldn't decide whether marketing sat with the region or the center; assigning a single D ended years of ping-pong between HQ and country managers.
- **Example — vendor selection:** Engineering is R, Security is A, the CTO is D, Procurement is P, and affected team leads are I — so the security veto is explicit and the CTO's commit is final.
author: Bain & Company (Paul Rogers & Marcia Blenko)
when_to_use: When a cross-functional decision keeps stalling or getting reopened because nobody knows who actually owns the call.
when_not_to_use: For fast, low-stakes, or single-owner decisions where the role-mapping overhead exceeds the decision's value.
diagram_anchors: **RAPID**, devised by Bain & Company (Paul Rogers & Marcia Blenko in HBR's "Who Has the D?", 2006), assigns five distinct roles in a complex decision so exactly one person owns the call and the rest know their lane.

name: Cost of Delay
summary: Reinertsen's economic lens: quantifies the value lost per week of NOT shipping a feature now, turning urgency into money.
body_md:
- **Cost of Delay (CoD)**, popularized by Donald G. Reinertsen in *The Principles of Product Development Flow* (2009), quantifies the economic value lost per unit of time (usually per week) that a feature is *not* available — converting the fuzzy question "how urgent is this?" into a money-per-time number.
- **Why it exists** — most teams optimize for cost and effort because those are visible, while delay cost is invisible; CoD makes the invisible queue cost explicit so it can be traded off against the work itself.
- **The three value components** to estimate for each item:
	- **Value/revenue** — the upside the feature delivers once live (new revenue, saved cost, churn avoided).
	- **Time criticality** — how fast that value decays if you wait (a deadline, a seasonal window, a competitor moving).
	- **Risk reduction / opportunity enablement** — value from de-risking or unlocking future options, even without direct revenue.
- **Urgency profiles** — CoD is not always linear: it can be *steady* (loses $X every week forever), *deadline-driven* (cliff drop after a fixed date), or *expedited* (huge cost now, fading later). The shape changes the right sequence.
- **Feeds WSJF** — divide CoD by job duration to get **Weighted Shortest Job First** (WSJF = CoD ÷ duration); doing the highest-WSJF item first minimizes total economic loss across the queue.
- **Worked example — Spotify-style seasonal feature:** a "Wrapped" year-in-review ships value only in December; its CoD is near zero in March but spikes to a cliff in late November — so it should jump the queue in Q4 even if "smaller" features look cheaper.
- **Worked example — payments bug vs. polish:** a checkout failure leaking $8k/week has a steady CoD of $8k; a UI polish item with no decay has a CoD near $0 — CoD says fix the leak first even though both are "one-sprint" jobs.
author: Donald G. Reinertsen
when_to_use: When sequencing a backlog of time-sensitive work and you need a common economic unit to compare apples-to-oranges items.
when_not_to_use: When value or time decay cannot be credibly estimated, where false precision can mislead more than gut feel.
diagram_anchors: **Cost of Delay (CoD)**, popularized by Donald G. Reinertsen in *The Principles of Product Development Flow* (2009), quantifies the economic value lost per unit of time

name: Bullseye Framework
summary: Weinberg & Mares's concentric-rings method: find the one traction channel that drives growth by ranking, testing, and focusing across 19 channels.
body_md:
- The **Bullseye Framework**, popularized by Gabriel Weinberg and Justin Mares in *Traction* (2015), is a five-step method for finding the single traction channel that will drive growth, chosen from 19 candidate channels and visualized as concentric rings (outer = all options, middle = the promising few, inner = the one channel that works).
- **The 19 traction channels** are the candidate set you draw from: viral marketing, PR, unconventional PR, SEM, social & display ads, offline ads, SEO, content marketing, email marketing, engineering as marketing, target-market blogs, business development, sales, affiliate programs, existing platforms, trade shows, offline events, speaking engagements, and community building.
- **Step 1 — Brainstorm (outer ring):** for *every* one of the 19 channels, write down at least one reasonable, specific idea for how you'd use it — no dismissing channels before you've thought them through.
- **Step 2 — Rank:** sort channels into three columns — **A** (most promising right now), **B** (possible), **C** (long shots) — so you triage without prematurely killing options.
- **Step 3 — Prioritize (middle ring):** pick your top ~3 channels from column A to actually test.
- **Step 4 — Test:** run cheap, fast, parallel experiments on those 3 to learn — roughly how much will customers cost, how many customers are available, and are these the right customers — without over-investing.
- **Step 5 — Focus (inner ring):** whichever test shows real traction, pour resources into that single channel and ride it until it stops working, then re-run the bullseye.
- **Example — DuckDuckGo:** Weinberg's own search engine found PR and word-of-mouth (not paid search ads) as its bullseye channel. **Example — early Mint** leaned on content marketing and target-market blogs; **Codecademy** used engineering-as-marketing (free coding tools) as its inner-ring channel.
author: Gabriel Weinberg & Justin Mares
when_to_use: When a startup needs to systematically choose which acquisition channel to bet on instead of guessing or copying competitors.
when_not_to_use: When you already have a proven, scaling channel — the bullseye is for finding traction, not optimizing a known winner.
diagram_anchors: The **Bullseye Framework**, popularized by Gabriel Weinberg and Justin Mares in *Traction* (2015), is a five-step method for finding the single traction channel that will drive growth

name: Goodhart's Law
aliases: When a measure becomes a target
summary: Goodhart's failure-mode taxonomy: once you target a proxy metric, optimization decouples it from the real goal.
body_md:
- **Goodhart's Law**, coined by economist **Charles Goodhart** (1975) and sharpened by Marilyn Strathern's gloss, states that *"when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure"* — the act of optimizing a proxy for a goal corrupts the proxy's link to that goal.
- **Why it happens:** a metric is only ever a *proxy* for the true outcome. Light optimization is fine; hard optimization pushes the system into regions where proxy and goal diverge, and rational agents exploit the gap.
- **Manheim & Garrabrant (2018)** give a taxonomy of four distinct mechanisms by which a target gets gamed:
	- **Regressional** — the proxy equals the goal plus noise, so the records you celebrate are won by luck, not merit; selecting the metric's top performers selects for favourable noise (regression to the mean).
	- **Extremal** — optimizing to extreme values leaves the ordinary region where proxy and goal were correlated; the relationship breaks down exactly where you pushed hardest.
	- **Causal** — you act on a non-causal correlation, so intervening on the proxy moves the number without moving the goal (or moves the goal the wrong way).
	- **Adversarial** — agents who know the metric optimize it deliberately, decoupling the number from the outcome on purpose.
- **Example — support tickets:** target "average handle time" and agents close tickets fast without resolving them (adversarial); the true goal, customer problems solved, drops while the dashboard improves.
- **Example — Wells Fargo:** an aggressive cross-sell metric ("accounts per customer") drove employees to open ~3.5M unauthorized accounts — a textbook adversarial Goodhart failure that cost the bank billions.
- **Example — growth metrics:** chasing raw "signups" (extremal) attracts incentivized, never-activating users; the metric soars while activated, retained users — the thing signups was a proxy for — stays flat.
author: Charles Goodhart
when_to_use: When you are setting or evaluating a metric target (OKR, north-star, growth KPI) and want to stress-test how it could be gamed.
when_not_to_use: When you have no measurement-driven incentive at all — it diagnoses the corruption of targeted metrics, it does not help you choose what to build.
diagram_anchors: states that *"when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure"*

name: Guardrail Metrics
summary: Kohavi's experiment safety net: pair a primary success metric with guardrails that must not regress, bounding what a "win" may cost.
body_md:
- **Guardrail Metrics** (formalized by Ron Kohavi and colleagues at Microsoft/Bing, and central to Airbnb's "Experiment Guardrails") are secondary metrics that an experiment must NOT regress, ensuring a win on the primary metric is not bought at an unacceptable cost.
- **Primary metric** — the single success metric the test is designed to move (e.g. checkout conversion, signups, revenue per user). You ship if and only if it improves AND every guardrail holds.
- **Guardrail (counter) metrics** — secondary metrics you are unwilling to harm, each with a pre-declared bound. A variant that crosses the bound is rejected even if the primary metric wins.
	- **Quality / performance guardrails** — page-load latency, crash rate, error rate. Faster primary numbers that ship a slower site are a false win.
	- **Trust / satisfaction guardrails** — complaint rate, unsubscribes, support tickets, churn and retention. Catches dark-pattern wins that juice today and bleed tomorrow.
	- **Validity guardrails** — Sample Ratio Mismatch (SRM): if the actual traffic split diverges from the intended split (e.g. 49/51 vs 50/50), the experiment is broken and its result is untrustworthy regardless of the primary metric.
- **The rule** — declare the primary metric and the guardrail bounds BEFORE the test runs, so you can't rationalize a regression after seeing the numbers.
- **Example — Bing/Microsoft:** any ranking change that lifts clicks must hold server response time; a slower result page measurably loses revenue, so latency is a hard guardrail.
- **Example — checkout funnel:** a one-click upsell raises average order value (primary) but lifts refund and complaint rates (guardrail) past their bound → the variant is rejected despite the revenue lift.
- **Example — email program:** a more aggressive send cadence raises open-driven conversions but trips the unsubscribe guardrail, signaling the win is borrowed against future reachability.
author: Ron Kohavi
when_to_use: When running A/B tests and you need to ensure a metric win does not silently degrade speed, trust, retention, or test validity.
when_not_to_use: When you have no live experiment platform or sufficient traffic to read secondary metrics with statistical confidence.
diagram_anchors: are secondary metrics that an experiment must NOT regress, ensuring a win on the primary metric is not bought at an unacceptable cost.

name: Vanity Vs. Actionable Metrics
summary: Eric Ries's quality test for metrics: separates flattering totals from numbers that drive a decision.
body_md:
- A test popularized by **Eric Ries** (*The Lean Startup*) that asks of any number: does moving it change how you act? If not, it is a vanity metric, however impressive it looks.
- **Vanity metric** — flattering, cumulative, and almost always rising (total signups, lifetime pageviews, raw "hits", follower count). It demonstrates no cause and effect, so a rise tells you nothing about what to do next.
- **Actionable metric** — ties a specific change to a specific result, so you can decide. Usually expressed as a **rate, ratio, or per-user value** (conversion %, ARPU, week-2 retention) rather than a running total.
- **Cohort over cumulative** — measure how one group of users behaves over time instead of pooling everyone, so improvements aren't masked by the inflow of new sign-ups.
- **The three A's** — Ries's test for a good metric: **Actionable** (clear cause → effect, ideally isolated by an A/B split test), **Accessible** (the team can read and understand it, not buried in a data warehouse), **Auditable** (you can trace a number back to real users, not a spreadsheet artifact).
- **Worked example — IMVU:** Ries's team watched total registered users climb while the business stalled; switching to per-cohort conversion and revenue-per-customer (set via split tests) exposed that new features weren't actually moving behavior.
- **Worked example — a media app** celebrating "10M cumulative downloads" learns nothing actionable; the actionable cut is *day-7 retention by install cohort*, which reveals whether each release improves stickiness.
- **Worked example — SaaS funnel:** "150k total leads" is vanity; **trial→paid conversion rate this month** is actionable because a pricing or onboarding change visibly moves it.
author: Eric Ries
when_to_use: Choosing or auditing a metric before you let a team or board steer by it.
when_not_to_use: Some "vanity" totals are still legitimate billing or compliance facts, so don't discard a number just because it only goes up.
diagram_anchors: A test popularized by **Eric Ries** (*The Lean Startup*) that asks of any number: does moving it change how you act?

name: Decoy Effect
summary: Huber, Payne & Puto's choice-architecture lever: a deliberately inferior third option steers buyers toward the plan it's dominated by.
body_md:
- The **Decoy Effect** (Huber, Payne & Puto, 1982) is the finding that adding a third, *asymmetrically dominated* option to a choice set shifts demand toward the option that dominates the decoy — a structural way to steer selection without altering any real plan.
- **Asymmetric dominance** — the decoy is the engine. It must be *clearly worse* than your **target** option on every attribute, yet *not* clearly worse than the **competitor** option. It loses cleanly to one, ties or muddles against the other.
- **Target** — the option you want chosen. The decoy is positioned so the target looks like an obvious, no-brainer upgrade right next to it.
- **Competitor** — the cheaper/simpler option the target was previously losing share to. The decoy doesn't touch it directly; it just makes the target shine by comparison.
- **Mechanism** — people struggle to compare options across different dimensions (price vs. size vs. features). A dominated decoy gives an *easy* comparison (decoy vs. target), and that easy win pulls the whole decision toward the target. It violates the "regularity" axiom of rational choice: adding an option *raises* an existing option's share.
- **Example — The Economist (Ariely's case):** Web-only $59; Print-only $125; Print+Web $125. The Print-only tier is the decoy: nobody picks it (it's dominated by Print+Web at the same price), but its presence drove most buyers to the $125 bundle. Remove it and far more chose the cheap web-only option.
- **Example — popcorn / coffee sizing:** Small $3, Large $7 splits evenly. Add a Medium at $6.50 (barely cheaper, much smaller than Large) and the Large's share jumps — the medium is the decoy that makes Large feel like the deal.
- **Example — SaaS plan pages:** A rarely-chosen middle tier priced just under the premium tier but with far fewer seats/features makes the premium tier read as the obvious value, lifting upgrades.
author: Joel Huber, John W. Payne & Christopher Puto
when_to_use: When you control the choice set (a pricing page or plan tiering) and want to nudge buyers toward a target plan without changing it.
when_not_to_use: When buyers compare against external offers or the decoy reads as a trick — it backfires and erodes trust.
diagram_anchors: The **Decoy Effect** (Huber, Payne & Puto, 1982) is the finding that adding a third, *asymmetrically dominated* option to a choice set shifts demand toward the option that dominates the decoy

name: Good-Better-Best Pricing
summary: Rafi Mohammed's three-tier ladder: package a product into Good, Better, and Best versions to capture a wider range of willingness-to-pay.
body_md:
- **Good-Better-Best (G-B-B)**, popularized by pricing strategist Rafi Mohammed in his 2018 HBR article, is a packaging architecture that splits one product into three priced tiers so a single offer can serve price-sensitive, mainstream, and premium buyers at once.
- **Good** — a stripped-down, lower-priced version that attracts new and price-sensitive customers (and blunts cheaper rivals). Build it by *fencing*: removing or limiting features from today's product rather than degrading it gratuitously.
- **Better** — the mainstream, full-featured offer that keeps existing customers happy. It is the intended default and usually the **anchor** the other tiers are read against.
- **Best** — a premium, feature-laden version that captures customers with high willingness-to-pay who will spend more for status, capacity, or extras. Its price also makes Better look reasonable (the **anchoring / compromise effect**).
- **Mechanism** — three jobs at once: *capture more of the demand curve* (low and high WTP), *anchor* choice toward the profitable middle, and create a built-in *upsell path* as customers grow.
- **How to build it** — start from your current product as Better; subtract to create Good (clear fences customers understand); add genuinely valued extras to create Best; then test names, feature splits, and price gaps before launch.
- **Examples**
	- **Apple iPhone** — the standard, Plus/Pro, and Pro Max line is a textbook G-B-B ladder, with storage tiers nested inside each.
	- **Slack** — Free (Good), Pro (Better), Business+ / Enterprise Grid (Best) gate message history, admin controls, and compliance.
	- **Car washes & airlines** — basic / deluxe / works packages and Economy / Premium Economy / Business mirror the same three-rung structure.
author: Rafi Mohammed
when_to_use: When one offer leaves money on the table across a span of customer willingness-to-pay and you want a self-service upsell ladder.
when_not_to_use: When buyers are homogeneous in needs and budget, or when added tiers create paralyzing choice and operational overhead that outweigh the captured margin.
diagram_anchors: **Good-Better-Best (G-B-B)**, popularized by pricing strategist Rafi Mohammed in his 2018 HBR article, is a packaging architecture that splits one product into three priced tiers

name: Aesthetic-Usability Effect
summary: Kurosu & Kashimura's perception bias: users judge attractive designs as more usable and forgive their minor flaws.
body_md:
- The **Aesthetic-Usability Effect** is a perception bias — first measured by Masaaki Kurosu and Kaori Kashimura (Hitachi, CHI 1995) and popularized by Don Norman — where users perceive aesthetically pleasing designs as easier to use and tolerate minor usability problems in them.
- **The 1995 study** — across 26 ATM-layout variants rated by 252 participants, perceived beauty correlated more strongly with *perceived* ease of use than actual ease of use did. How good it looked drove how usable people *thought* it was.
- **Positive affect mechanism** — attractive interfaces trigger positive emotion, which makes users more patient, more trusting, and more creative at problem-solving, so they push through small friction instead of giving up or blaming the UI.
- **The forgiveness band** — a polished design earns a buffer: the same minor flaw (a slightly hidden button, an extra tap) is excused in a beautiful UI but read as "broken" in a plain one. Beauty buys tolerance, not immunity.
- **The PM trap** — that same halo can mask genuine usability defects during testing: participants rate a gorgeous prototype highly while quietly failing the task, so qualitative praise overstates real performance.
- **Example — Apple:** decades of fit-and-finish (typography, spacing, motion) make users perceive Apple software as effortless and forgive quirks competitors would get roasted for.
- **Example — Mailchimp:** playful illustration and warm copy make a complex marketing tool *feel* approachable, lowering perceived difficulty before a user has done anything.
- **Example — fintech onboarding:** two banking apps with identical flows test very differently; the one with cleaner visuals and smooth transitions scores higher on "easy to use" even when completion times match.
author: Masaaki Kurosu & Kaori Kashimura
when_to_use: When deciding how much visual polish a flow needs and how to read usability-test reactions to attractive vs. rough prototypes.
when_not_to_use: When you need an objective measure of task success or error rates — beauty inflates perceived usability and can hide real defects.
diagram_anchors: The **Aesthetic-Usability Effect** is a perception bias

name: Affordances and Signifiers
summary: Don Norman's design lens: separate what an object lets you do (affordance) from the cue that tells you how (signifier).
body_md:
- **Affordances and signifiers** is Don Norman's design distinction (popularized in *The Design of Everyday Things*) that splits the action a thing makes possible from the perceivable cue telling the user that action exists and how to do it.
- **Affordance** — the relationship between an object's properties and a user's abilities that determines what actions are *possible*. A flat plate affords pushing; a chair affords sitting; a slider affords dragging. Affordances exist whether or not anyone notices them. The term comes from psychologist J.J. Gibson; Norman borrowed it for design.
- **Signifier** — any perceivable indicator that communicates *where* and *how* to act: a "PUSH" label, a handle's shape, an underline on a link, a subtle shadow on a button. Signifiers are the part the designer most controls; they make a hidden affordance discoverable.
- **Perceived vs. real affordance** — what matters for usability is the affordance the user *believes* exists. A faked 3-D button on a screen has no physical affordance, but its signifier makes users perceive "press me," which is enough.
- **Design rule** — when an action fails, ask: is the affordance missing (the thing genuinely can't do it) or is the *signifier* missing (it can, but nothing tells the user)? Most "confusing UI" is a signifier problem, not a capability problem.
- **Example — Norman doors:** A glass door with a vertical pull bar on a side you must *push* affords both push and pull; the bar is a signifier that lies. A flat metal plate signifies "push" and removes the guesswork.
- **Example — flat web buttons:** When iOS/Material went flat, many tappable elements lost their shadow signifiers; users stopped recognizing buttons even though the tap affordance was unchanged — teams added subtle elevation, color, and borders back as signifiers.
- **Example — pull-to-refresh:** The affordance (drag-down reloads) is invisible; a spinner and arrow appearing as you tug are the signifiers that teach it.
author: Don Norman
when_to_use: When users can't find or correctly trigger an action and you need to diagnose whether the capability or its cue is missing.
when_not_to_use: When the usability problem is about overall flow, information architecture, or content rather than the discoverability of a single action.
diagram_anchors: **Affordances and signifiers** is Don Norman's design distinction (popularized in *The Design of Everyday Things*) that splits the action a thing makes possible from the perceivable cue telling the user that action exists and how to do it.

name: Atomic Design
summary: Brad Frost's compositional hierarchy: build design systems from five nested levels — atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, pages.
body_md:
- **Atomic Design**, coined by Brad Frost (2016), is a methodology for building design systems by composing UI from five nested levels so interfaces stay consistent and are assembled from reusable parts.
- **Atoms** — the smallest indivisible building blocks: a button, input, label, color token, or font. They carry the base styling but aren't very useful on their own.
- **Molecules** — small groups of atoms bonded into a functional unit. A search form = label atom + input atom + button atom; the unit now does one job well.
- **Organisms** — relatively complex sections built from molecules and atoms: a site header combining a logo, primary nav, and search molecule. Organisms are recognizable, standalone interface regions.
- **Templates** — page-level layouts that arrange organisms into a structure, using placeholder content to define the skeleton and content hierarchy without real data.
- **Pages** — specific instances of a template filled with real, representative content; the level where you stress-test the system against edge cases (long names, missing images, empty states).
- **Not linear** — Frost stresses it's a mental model, not a step-by-step pipeline: you see an interface as a whole and as a collection of parts simultaneously, and you move between levels freely.
- **Examples** — Shopify's Polaris, Salesforce's Lightning, and the U.S. Web Design System all organize tokens → components → patterns → pages along this atoms-up hierarchy. Frost's own demo, Pattern Lab, renders the same components at every level so a team can review an atom and the page that uses it side by side.
author: Brad Frost
when_to_use: Building or restructuring a reusable design system / component library that designers and engineers share.
when_not_to_use: A small one-off page or throwaway prototype where the overhead of a layered component taxonomy isn't worth it.
diagram_anchors: **Atomic Design**, coined by Brad Frost (2016), is a methodology for building design systems by composing UI from five nested levels

name: Doherty Threshold
summary: Doherty & Thadani's latency rule: keep system response under ~400ms or you break the user's flow and productivity.
body_md:
- **Doherty Threshold** — Walter Doherty and Arvind Thadani (IBM, 1982) found that productivity soars when system response time stays under **~400ms**, the point at which neither human nor computer waits on the other.
- **The 400ms line** — below it, interaction feels instantaneous and even "addictive"; above it, the user's attention drifts, flow breaks, and perceived effort climbs. The figure replaced the older 2-second industry standard.
- **Why it works** — sub-400ms responses keep the user inside their working-memory loop, so they stay engaged and complete more in less time; the speed itself becomes a reason to keep using the product.
- **Perceived vs. actual time** — you can honor the threshold by being genuinely fast *or* by managing perception:
	- **Optimistic UI** — render the result immediately and reconcile with the server in the background (a "liked" heart fills instantly).
	- **Skeleton screens & progress** — show structure or motion so the wait reads as shorter than a blank spinner.
	- **Preloading & prefetching** — fetch the next likely state before the user asks for it.
- **Worked examples:**
	- **Google Search** instant-results and autocomplete fire keystroke-by-keystroke under the threshold, so searching feels like thinking.
	- **Facebook / Instagram** like and comment actions update optimistically on tap, well under 400ms, regardless of network round-trip.
	- **Figma / Linear** local-first rendering and prefetch keep navigation and edits instant, which is a core part of why both feel premium.
author: Walter J. Doherty & Arvind J. Thadani
when_to_use: When deciding how fast an interaction must feel and where to spend latency budget on a core flow.
when_not_to_use: When the bottleneck is unavoidable heavy computation where a well-designed progress/optimistic state matters more than raw speed.
diagram_anchors: Walter Doherty and Arvind Thadani (IBM, 1982) found that productivity soars when system response time stays under **~400ms**

name: Emotional Design
summary: Don Norman's three-level lens: products earn love at the visceral, behavioral, and reflective levels at once.
body_md:
- **Emotional Design**, named by Don Norman in his 2003 book, says products are processed at three levels of the brain — visceral, behavioral, and reflective — and great products satisfy all three rather than just working.
- **Visceral — first look.** The immediate, pre-conscious gut reaction to appearance, feel, sound, and texture. It happens before thought and decides whether someone is drawn in at all.
- **Behavioral — in use.** The pleasure and effectiveness of actually using the thing: does it do the job, is it understandable, does it feel good in the hand and the flow. This is where usability lives.
- **Reflective — after.** The slowest, most conscious level: meaning, memory, self-image, and the story the product lets you tell about yourself. It governs long-term attachment, brand, and word of mouth.
- The levels can conflict — a beautiful object (visceral) can be frustrating to use (behavioral), and people will still forgive it if the reflective payoff is high enough. Strong products align all three.
- **Apple iPhone** — visceral in the unboxing and glass, behavioral in direct-manipulation gestures, reflective in the identity of owning one.
- **Nest Thermostat** — a visceral glowing ring, behavioral auto-learning that removes effort, reflective pride in being a responsible, modern homeowner.
- **Duolingo** — visceral mascot and color, behavioral bite-sized lessons, reflective streaks and identity as "someone learning a language."
author: Don Norman
when_to_use: When deciding why a usable product still feels flat and what would make users actually love and recommend it.
when_not_to_use: When the basic task is broken or unusable — fix core usability before chasing visceral delight or reflective meaning.
diagram_anchors: **Emotional Design**, named by Don Norman in his 2003 book, says products are processed at three levels of the brain

name: Goal-Gradient Effect
summary: Hull's motivation curve: people work harder as a goal gets closer, and an artificial head-start speeds completion.
body_md:
- **Goal-Gradient Effect** — psychologist Clark Hull (1932), studying rats running a maze, found that effort intensifies as the distance to a reward shrinks; the closer the goal, the faster you push. Humans show the same acceleration toward visible goals.
- **The gradient** — motivation is not flat across a task; it rises non-linearly near the finish. The first step feels heavy, the last step feels urgent. Surfacing *how close the end is* harnesses that late-stage surge.
- **Endowed Progress (Nunes & Drèze, 2006)** — you can move people up the curve by *granting* artificial progress at the start. A "buy 8, get 1 free" card pre-stamped with 2 free stamps (so it reads "buy 10, get 1 free, 2 already done") out-completes a clean 8-stamp card — same real work, but the head-start reframes the user as *already underway* rather than *at zero*.
- **Design levers** — make the gradient legible and tilt the start:
	- **Progress bars & step counters** — show the goal and how much remains ("Step 3 of 4", "80% to your reward").
	- **Endowed head-start** — credit the user some progress for free at signup so they begin partway, not empty.
	- **Small early wins** — front-load an easy first milestone so the gradient engages quickly.
- **Worked examples:**
	- **LinkedIn profile strength** — the "All-Star" meter and "complete your profile" nudges exploit proximity-to-goal; completion jumps as the bar fills.
	- **Starbucks Rewards / coffee punch cards** — visible stars-to-reward make the last few purchases feel urgent; many programs pre-credit a star or two as an endowed head-start.
	- **Duolingo lesson bars** — a per-lesson progress bar and streak counter turn "almost done" into a reason to finish one more screen.
author: Clark L. Hull
when_to_use: When designing a multi-step flow, loyalty program, or onboarding where you want to lift completion by making progress and proximity to the goal visible.
when_not_to_use: When the goal itself has no real value to the user — fake progress on a hollow reward just teaches people to ignore your progress UI.
diagram_anchors: psychologist Clark Hull (1932), studying rats running a maze, found that effort intensifies as the distance to a reward shrinks

name: Jakob's Law
aliases: Jakob's Law of Internet User Experience
summary: Jakob Nielsen's convention-over-novelty principle: users prefer your product to work like the others they already know.
body_md:
- **Jakob's Law**, coined by usability pioneer Jakob Nielsen (Nielsen Norman Group; collected in Jon Yablonski's *Laws of UX*), states that users spend most of their time on *other* products, so they prefer yours to work the same way as all the ones they already know — convention over novelty.
- **Mental-model transfer** — users carry expectations built on familiar products into yours the moment it looks similar; meeting those expectations lets them focus on the task instead of learning your interface.
- **Leverage existing models** — reusing established patterns (logo top-left returns home, cart top-right, search in the header, hamburger = menu) lowers cognitive load and is usually the right default, not laziness.
- **Manage change friction** — when you must break a convention, minimize discord by letting users keep the familiar version for a limited time, previewing the new one before committing.
- **Convention is a budget, not a cage** — spend your novelty where it differentiates the core value; pay the conventional "tax" everywhere else so the unfamiliar part stands out and is learnable.
- **Examples:**
	- *E-commerce checkout* — Amazon's layout (cart top-right, single dominant CTA, address-then-payment steps) became the convention; new stores that reshuffle it see drop-off, so most clone the flow.
	- *YouTube's 2017 redesign* — rolled out Material Design as an opt-in preview with a one-click revert, easing users into a new mental model rather than forcing it overnight.
	- *Settings gear & hamburger menu* — icons users learned on iOS/Android/Gmail; a product inventing its own glyphs forces relearning for no gain.
author: Jakob Nielsen
when_to_use: Designing a new flow or interface where users arrive with expectations from incumbent products.
when_not_to_use: When a genuinely better paradigm justifies the relearning cost, or when convention itself is the source of the problem you are solving.
diagram_anchors: states that users spend most of their time on *other* products, so they prefer yours to work the same way as all the ones they already know

name: Miller's Law
body_md:
- Miller's Law, popularized by cognitive psychologist George A. Miller in 1956, states that the average person can hold only about **seven (plus or minus two)** items in their working memory at once.
- The practical takeaway is **chunking**: rather than treating raw capacity as fixed, group information into meaningful units so that each "chunk" counts as one item — letting people effectively hold far more.
	- **Capacity is about chunks, not bits** — a chunk can be a single digit or a whole grouped concept, so good grouping expands what fits.
	- **Meaningful grouping beats raw recall** — familiar, structured units (a phone-number pattern, a word) are easier to retain than the same data ungrouped.
	- **Seven is a guide, not a hard limit** — it cautions against overload; it does not mandate exactly seven menu items or seven of anything.
- How to apply it in product design:
	- Break long strings (phone numbers, card numbers, codes) into grouped segments so they read and recall easily.
	- Organize navigation, settings, and forms into labeled sections so users scan a few groups instead of a long flat list.
	- Use progressive disclosure to reveal detail on demand rather than presenting everything at once.
- Concrete examples:
	- **Phone numbers**: 9283550172 is hard to hold, but "928-355-0172" splits it into three memorable chunks.
	- **Stripe / checkout forms**: card-number fields auto-format into 4-digit groups, easing entry and error-checking.
	- **Apple & Google settings menus**: long option lists are grouped under labeled categories so each screen presents a handful of chunks, not dozens of flat rows.
author: George A. Miller
summary: Miller's working-memory rule: people hold only about 7 (±2) items at once, so chunk information into meaningful groups.
when_to_use: When grouping content, fields, options, or navigation so users can hold and process them without overloading short-term memory.
when_not_to_use: When the literal "7" is treated as a hard cap for unrelated UI elements rather than a guide to chunk related information.
diagram_anchors: Miller's Law, popularized by cognitive psychologist George A. Miller in 1956, states that the average person can hold only about **seven (plus or minus two)** items in their working memory at once.

name: Peak-End Rule
summary: Kahneman's memory bias: people judge an experience by its most intense moment and its ending, not the average.
body_md:
- **The Peak-End Rule**, demonstrated by Daniel Kahneman with Barbara Fredrickson, Charles Schreiber, and Donald Redelmeier (*When More Pain Is Preferred to Less*, 1993), holds that people retrospectively judge an experience almost entirely by how it felt at its most intense point (the **peak**) and at its **end**, roughly averaging those two and ignoring everything else.
- **Duration neglect** — how *long* the experience lasted barely affects the remembered rating; a longer flow with a gentler finish can be recalled as better than a shorter, sharper one.
- **The remembering self vs. the experiencing self** — the version of the user that decides whether to return is the one holding the *memory*, not the one who lived every second; you optimize the memory, not the timeline average.
- **Engineer the peak** — deliberately design one standout moment of value, surprise, or relief (an "aha", a win, a delightful microinteraction) rather than smearing mediocre polish evenly across the flow.
- **Nail the ending** — end on a high or a relief, since the final moment is disproportionately weighted; a strong close can rescue an otherwise rough experience, and a weak close can taint a good one.
- **Examples:**
	- *IKEA* — a long, sometimes tiring store journey ends at the checkout with a cheap soft-serve ice cream or a hot-dog — a low-cost positive ending that lifts the remembered visit.
	- *Duolingo* — a lesson ends with a celebratory streak animation, XP burst, and mascot praise; the recalled feeling is "win", which pulls users back the next day.
	- *Uber* — the peak (a clean map, ETA certainty) and the end (a frictionless cashless exit — no awkward payment moment) are engineered, so the remembered ride beats the experienced traffic.
author: Daniel Kahneman
when_to_use: Shaping the emotional arc of a journey — deciding where to invest delight and how to land the ending of an onboarding, support, or checkout flow.
when_not_to_use: When the metric you actually care about is in-the-moment task efficiency or duration (e.g. latency-critical flows), where engineering a "peak" adds friction without improving recalled value.
diagram_anchors: holds that people retrospectively judge an experience almost entirely by how it felt at its most intense point (the **peak**) and at its **end**

name: Progressive Disclosure
summary: Nielsen's layered-reveal pattern: show the common options up front and defer advanced ones to a secondary view, cutting load without losing power.
body_md:
- **Progressive Disclosure**, named by Jakob Nielsen (NN/g, 2006), is an interaction pattern that shows only the most important and common options on the initial screen and defers advanced or rarely-used ones to a secondary screen revealed on request — so the interface is easy to learn yet still fully powerful.
- **Split by frequency, not feature parity:** the cut line is "how often / how many users need this," not "is it advanced" — the goal is that the default screen covers the dominant task path.
- **Initial layer — the common few:** surface the options the majority reach for, kept small enough to scan and decide quickly, lowering cognitive load and error rates.
- **Secondary layer — the advanced rest:** every deferred control stays one deliberate action away (a "More options", disclosure triangle, "Advanced…" panel, or a second step), so no power is removed — only postponed.
- **Reveal on intent:** the user pulls the second layer open when they need it; the system never forces the full surface on someone who doesn't.
- **Distinct from staged disclosure:** progressive disclosure is a two-tier common/advanced split; *staged* disclosure spreads one task across a sequence of steps (a wizard). Don't confuse hiding-by-importance with chunking-a-flow.
- **The failure mode:** if you defer something users actually need often, you've created the "where is it?" hunt — the cost of a wrong split is a frustrated search, so base the line on real usage data, not designer taste.
- **Examples:** *Google Search* shows a single box and hides everything else behind "Advanced search." *macOS / iOS Print* dialogs open minimal and expand to copies, paper, scaling behind "Show Details." *Gmail's* compose window keeps To/Subject/Body up front and tucks Cc, Bcc, formatting, and scheduling behind disclosure controls. *Photoshop / Figma* expose core tools and bury rarely-touched settings in expandable panels.
author: Jakob Nielsen
when_to_use: Designing an interface where a few options serve most users but power features must stay reachable.
when_not_to_use: When usage is flat across options or experts need everything at once — hiding then just adds clicks and a hunt.
diagram_anchors: shows only the most important and common options on the initial screen and defers advanced or rarely-used ones to a secondary screen revealed on request

name: Service Blueprinting
summary: Shostack's layered service map: extends a journey with the line of visibility and the back-stage people, processes and systems that deliver it.
body_md:
- **Service Blueprinting**, introduced by banker and service-marketing pioneer G. Lynn Shostack in *Designing Services That Deliver* (HBR, 1984), is a diagram that maps a customer journey across stacked swimlanes and adds the back-stage work — the people, processes and systems — that produce each visible moment, separated by a **line of visibility**.
- **Physical evidence** — the tangible touchpoints the customer encounters at each step (a confirmation email, a counter, the app screen, a receipt).
- **Customer actions** — what the customer actually does, step by step, left to right along the timeline.
- **Front-stage (onstage) actions** — what customer-facing staff or interfaces do in direct contact with the customer, sitting just above the **line of interaction**.
- **Line of visibility** — the central dividing line: everything above it the customer can see; everything below it is hidden delivery work.
- **Back-stage (backstage) actions** — employee and system actions the customer never sees but which directly enable the front-stage moment (a barista grinding beans, a fulfilment pick).
- **Support processes & systems** — the underlying infrastructure, third parties and internal systems (payment gateway, inventory DB, logistics partner) below the **line of internal interaction**.
- The blueprint reads left-to-right as time and top-to-bottom as depth, so each customer step has a vertical column linking the visible moment to everything required to deliver it.
- **Example — Starbucks order:** customer actions (queue, order, pay, collect); front-stage (cashier greets, barista calls name); back-stage (barista pulls shot, restocks); support (POS, supply chain, loyalty system). Blueprinting surfaces that a slow morning isn't a "friendly staff" problem but a back-stage staffing and machine-throughput problem.
- **Example — telehealth visit:** front-stage video consult sits above the line; below it sit triage nurses, e-prescription routing, lab integrations and insurance eligibility checks — the blueprint exposes the hand-off where a prescription stalls.
author: G. Lynn Shostack
when_to_use: When a service touches multiple channels, hand-offs or teams and you need to see what the customer can't to fix delivery failures.
when_not_to_use: When you only need to understand customer-visible perception and emotion — a plain journey map or empathy map is lighter.
diagram_anchors: is a diagram that maps a customer journey across stacked swimlanes and adds the back-stage work

name: Tesler's Law
summary: Larry Tesler's conservation law: every system has irreducible complexity — the only choice is whether the user or the system absorbs it.
body_md:
- **Tesler's Law** (the Law of Conservation of Complexity, coined by Xerox PARC's Larry Tesler in the mid-1980s) states that every application has an inherent, irreducible amount of complexity that cannot be wished away — it can only be shifted between the system/team and the user.
- **Complexity is conserved** — like a waterbed, press it down in the interface and it pops up in the code, or vice versa. The total stays fixed; only the *location* moves.
- **The core question** is *who absorbs it*:
	- **System/team absorbs** — engineers invest effort up front (smart defaults, auto-fill, hidden logic, fewer choices) so the user's job feels effortless.
	- **User absorbs** — the product ships simpler code but pushes manual steps, configuration, and choices onto the user.
- **The design principle**: for software used by many, shift complexity toward the team — your one-time engineering cost is amortized across millions of interactions, so the user should rarely pay it.
- **The honest caution**: complexity hidden from the user is not destroyed. It still lives in the code as edge cases and maintenance burden — the team trades user friction for engineering friction, not for nothing.
- Worked examples:
	- **Google search** — a single empty box hides spell-correction, ranking, and intent parsing; the system swallows enormous complexity so the user types a few words.
	- **TurboTax** — converts the labyrinth of tax law into a plain question-and-answer wizard; the product absorbs the rules so the filer never reads a form.
	- **Email "To" field** — someone must specify a valid recipient address. The app can auto-complete from contacts (system absorbs), but it cannot eliminate the need to *choose* a recipient — that residue is the user's irreducibly.
author: Larry Tesler
when_to_use: When deciding how to simplify a feature and you want to locate where its unavoidable complexity should live.
when_not_to_use: When the complexity is actually accidental and can be removed outright — Tesler's Law only governs the irreducible remainder.
diagram_anchors: **Tesler's Law** (the Law of Conservation of Complexity, coined by Xerox PARC's Larry Tesler in the mid-1980s) states that every application has an inherent, irreducible amount of complexity that cannot be wished away

name: Von Restorff Effect
summary: Von Restorff's isolation principle: among similar items, the one that differs most is the most noticed and remembered.
body_md:
- **Von Restorff Effect** (a.k.a. the **Isolation Effect**), described by psychologist Hedwig von Restorff in 1933, predicts that when several similar items are present, the one that differs most from the rest is the most likely to be noticed and remembered.
- **The mechanism — distinctiveness drives encoding:** memory and attention latch onto whatever breaks a pattern; a single deviating item stands out against a homogeneous field, so the contrast itself becomes the signal.
- **Isolate the target:** make the key action or item visually distinct from its neighbors using **color**, **size**, **shape**, **weight**, **whitespace**, or **motion** — any axis on which it departs from the surrounding set.
- **Use restraint — emphasis is a budget:** if everything is highlighted, nothing is. Each added accent dilutes the others, and over-emphasized elements can read as ads and get tuned out (banner blindness).
- **Guide attention, don't fight it:** pair isolation with a clear visual hierarchy so the eye lands on the distinctive element first, then the supporting choices.
- Example — **Pricing pages:** SaaS plans (Slack, Notion, Basecamp) render two or three tiers identically, then isolate the “recommended” tier with a colored border, a badge, and a filled button so it draws the eye and the click.
- Example — **Primary vs. secondary buttons:** a filled accent-colored “Save” next to a ghost-outline “Cancel” isolates the intended action; the desaturated secondary recedes by design.
- Example — **Spotify / app onboarding:** a single bright “Get started” against muted supporting copy, or a “New” badge on one menu item, channels attention to the one thing the product wants you to do next.
author: Hedwig von Restorff
when_to_use: When you need one element — a primary CTA, a recommended plan, a new feature — to be noticed and acted on.
when_not_to_use: When several elements genuinely deserve equal weight, since over-isolating everything cancels the effect entirely.
diagram_anchors: predicts that when several similar items are present, the one that differs most from the rest is the most likely to be noticed and remembered

name: Assumption Mapping
summary: Bland & Osterwalder's 2×2: surface a team's hidden bets, then test the high-importance, low-evidence "leap-of-faith" ones first.
body_md:
- **Assumption Mapping**, popularized by David J. Bland and Alexander Osterwalder in *Testing Business Ideas* (2019), is a team workshop that lists everything an idea must be true for it to work, then plots each belief on a 2×2 of **importance × evidence** so you test the riskiest assumptions before committing.
- **Surface assumptions across three lenses** — write each belief on a sticky under one of:
	- **Desirability** — do customers want it? (the need, the segment, the willingness to switch).
	- **Viability** — should we do it? (revenue, cost, margin, the business model holds).
	- **Feasibility** — can we build and deliver it? (tech, ops, partners, regulation).
- **Plot on the 2×2** — two axes:
	- **Importance** (vertical): if this turns out false, does the whole idea collapse, or just wobble?
	- **Evidence** (horizontal): how much real proof do we already have, versus opinion?
- **Read the quadrants** —
	- **Top-left = leap-of-faith assumptions** (important + little evidence): the dangerous unknowns — **design experiments for these first**.
	- **Top-right** (important + supported): de-risked, but keep monitoring.
	- **Bottom-left** (low-stakes + unproven): park; test later if at all.
	- **Bottom-right** (low-stakes + supported): ignore.
- **Worked example — a meal-kit subscription:** "busy professionals will pay $12/meal" lands top-left (kills the model if false, no proof) → run a smoke test; "we can source ingredients" is feasible-but-known → bottom-right.
- **Worked example — Airbnb early on:** the leap-of-faith desirability bet "strangers will sleep in each other's homes" sat top-left; everything hinged on it, so it was the first thing to validate, not the booking flow.
- The map turns a vague "let's build it" into a ranked **test backlog**, and forces the room to admit which beliefs are actually opinions wearing the costume of facts.
author: David J. Bland & Alexander Osterwalder
when_to_use: When a team is about to invest in an idea and needs to agree on which unproven beliefs are worth testing before building.
when_not_to_use: When the core assumptions are already evidenced or the decision is low-stakes — mapping then just adds ceremony.
diagram_anchors: plots each belief on a 2×2 of **importance × evidence** so you test the riskiest assumptions before committing

name: Concierge MVP
summary: Eric Ries's manual-first MVP: deliver the whole service by hand to a few real customers to validate value before you build any product.
body_md:
- A **Concierge MVP** (popularized by Eric Ries in *The Lean Startup*) manually delivers the full end-to-end service to a handful of real customers — no product built — so the team learns the workflow and validates demand before automating anything.
- **Overt, not faked.** The customer knows the service is being performed by hand; this differs from a Wizard-of-Oz MVP where the manual work is hidden behind a product-like façade.
- **Serve a few, not many.** You aren't trying to scale — you're trying to make one or a handful of customers genuinely happy and watch every step they go through.
- **Map the real workflow.** Doing the job by hand surfaces the actual steps, exceptions, and what customers truly value — knowledge you'd otherwise guess at when speccing software.
- **Automate last, and selectively.** Only after the manual version proves valuable do you write code — and you automate just the steps customers cared about, dropping the rest.
- **Worked example — Food on the Table.** Ries's canonical case: the startup began with a single customer who got a weekly in-person visit from the CEO, who reviewed local store deals and built recipes by hand. No technology existed. Only once the value was proven did they build software; it later scaled to a million customers.
- **Worked example — Airbnb's early hosts.** Founders manually photographed listings and hand-held early hosts and guests through bookings, learning the workflow and trust gaps before automating onboarding and payments.
- **Worked example — early Stripe ("Collison installation").** Founders would sit down and set up the integration by hand for early customers, learning every point of friction before building self-serve onboarding.
author: Eric Ries
when_to_use: When you want to learn the real end-to-end workflow and prove customers value the outcome before investing in software.
when_not_to_use: When the value is intrinsically the technology (scale, speed, algorithmic quality) so hand-delivery can't test what actually matters.
diagram_anchors: A **Concierge MVP** (popularized by Eric Ries in *The Lean Startup*) manually delivers the full end-to-end service to a handful of real customers

name: Fake Door Test
summary: Savoia-style pretotyping test: expose a button or page for an unbuilt feature and measure click intent to gauge real demand.
body_md:
- A **fake door test** (popularized within Alberto Savoia's *pretotyping*) exposes a button, link, or landing page for a feature that does **not exist yet** and measures who clicks or signs up, turning a guess about demand into observed behavior before any code is written.
- **Paint the door** — add the entry point (a menu item, CTA, pricing tier, or landing page) in the real product or a stand-in, styled exactly as if the feature were live.
- **Drive real traffic** — show it to genuine users in their natural flow, not a survey panel, so the click reflects intent-in-context rather than stated preference.
- **Measure revealed demand** — track click-through and follow-through (sign-up, email capture) against a pre-set threshold you defined before launching.
- **Show an honest curtain** — when the user opens the door, reveal a graceful “coming soon / notify me” state instead of a dead end, capturing leads and limiting the trust hit.
- **Decide** — high intent funds the build; weak intent kills it cheaply. Savoia frames this as testing **The Right It** before you build It right.
- *Example — Zynga* reportedly tested new game features as clickable buttons that led to a “not built yet” page, greenlighting only the ones users clicked.
- *Example — a SaaS team* adds an “Export to PDF” item to a menu; 28% of active users click within a week versus a 5% bar, so the feature jumps the roadmap.
- *Example — pricing* surfaces a fourth “Enterprise” tier with a Contact-us door to size demand for a plan that has no backing product yet.
author: Alberto Savoia
when_to_use: When you want evidence that a proposed feature is wanted before paying to build it.
when_not_to_use: When showing a non-functional door would damage trust or when ethics/regulation forbid promising something you cannot deliver.
diagram_anchors: A **fake door test** (popularized within Alberto Savoia's *pretotyping*) exposes a button, link, or landing page for a feature that does **not exist yet**

name: Five Whys
summary: Sakichi Toyoda's question ladder: ask "Why?" ~five times to move past symptoms to the root cause, then fix that cause.
body_md:
- **Five Whys** is a root-cause technique popularized by Sakichi Toyoda within the Toyota Production System (and adapted for startups by Eric Ries): for a given problem you ask "Why did this happen?", then ask "Why?" of that answer, repeating roughly five times until you reach a cause that, if fixed, stops the problem from recurring.
- **Start from a concrete symptom** — a specific failure that actually happened ("the site went down," "the customer churned"), not a vague complaint. Each answer must be a verifiable fact, not a guess.
- **Chain each "Why?" onto the previous answer** — the question targets the prior answer, so the inquiry drills downward instead of sideways, walking from technical symptom toward a process or human-system cause.
- **~Five is a heuristic, not a rule** — stop when you hit a cause you can actually act on; sometimes that's three Whys, sometimes seven. Going too shallow leaves a symptom; going too deep reaches "human nature" and loses actionability.
- **The root cause is usually a process gap, not a person** — well-run Five Whys lands on a missing check, an absent process, or unclear ownership. Eric Ries's twist: make a **proportional investment** at each level so fixes match the pain, and avoid the "Five Blames" failure mode where it devolves into finding someone to fault.
- **Worked example (Toyota, the canonical one):** A machine stopped. *Why?* The fuse blew from overload. *Why?* The bearing wasn't lubricated enough. *Why?* The lubrication pump wasn't pumping. *Why?* The pump shaft was worn. *Why?* No filter, so metal shavings got in. Root fix: add a filter — not just replace the fuse.
- **Worked example (startup, Ries):** A new release broke a feature for customers. *Why?* A bad server config shipped. *Why?* A subsystem failed silently. *Why?* An engineer made a mistake. *Why?* They weren't trained on it. *Why?* Their manager doesn't onboard new hires. Root fix: a training/onboarding process — a people-system cause, not "the engineer was careless."
author: Sakichi Toyoda
when_to_use: When a recurring defect, outage, or missed metric keeps coming back and you suspect you're treating symptoms, not causes.
when_not_to_use: For problems with multiple interacting causes or where a single linear chain oversimplifies (use a fishbone or fault tree instead).
diagram_anchors: **Five Whys** is a root-cause technique popularized by Sakichi Toyoda within the Toyota Production System

name: How Might We
summary: Basadur's reframing prompt: it turns a research insight into an optimistic, open-but-bounded "How might we…?" question that launches ideation.
body_md:
- **How Might We (HMW)** is a reframing technique — coined by Min Basadur at P&G and popularized by IDEO — that converts a user insight into a short, optimistically-phrased question ("How might we…?") which invites many solutions without prescribing one.
- **Start from an insight, not a feature** — take a synthesized observation or pain point from research (e.g. "travelers dread the long check-in line") rather than a half-baked solution.
- **Decode the three words** — each does work:
	- **How** — presupposes that solutions *do* exist, so the team starts hunting rather than debating feasibility.
	- **Might** — signals "this is a maybe," giving everyone permission to throw out wild ideas and defer judgment.
	- **We** — frames ideation as a collaborative team effort, not one hero's pitch.
- **Tune the scope (the Goldilocks test)** — too narrow ("how might we add a faster kiosk") smuggles in the answer; too broad ("how might we reinvent travel") boils the ocean. Aim for the bounded-but-open middle.
- **Generate several HMWs per insight** — vary the angle (amp the good, remove the bad, explore the opposite, question the assumption) to get a portfolio of framings before you ideate.
- **Worked examples:**
	- **P&G / Basadur** reframed a soap insight into "How might we make a more refreshing soap of our own?" — which led to the striped Coast bar.
	- **IDEO airport project:** the insight "security feels stressful" became "How might we help travelers feel calm and in control as they move through the airport?" — opening ideas far beyond shorter lines.
	- **Google Ventures Design Sprints** open Day 1 by turning sticky-note problems into a wall of HMW notes, then dot-voting the sharpest one to ideate against.
author: Min Basadur
when_to_use: Right after synthesizing research, to convert a pain or insight into a brief that opens divergent ideation.
when_not_to_use: When the problem is already well-scoped and you need to converge, decide, or build — HMW is a divergence opener, not a decision tool.
diagram_anchors: **How Might We (HMW)** is a reframing technique — coined by Min Basadur at P&G and popularized by IDEO — that converts a user insight into a short, optimistically-phrased question

name: Hypothesis-Driven Development
summary: O'Reilly & Gothelf's experiment loop: frame each feature as a falsifiable hypothesis, build the smallest test, and let evidence decide persevere or pivot.
body_md:
- **Hypothesis-Driven Development (HDD)**, popularized by Barry O'Reilly (ThoughtWorks) and Jeff Gothelf (Lean UX), reframes every feature as a falsifiable bet and treats the team's build pipeline as a continuous experiment engine rather than a feature factory.
- **Write the hypothesis** — state the bet in a fixed shape: "We **believe** [building this capability] for [these users] will achieve [this outcome]. We'll **know we're right** when we see [this measurable signal]." If you can't name the signal, you don't have a hypothesis yet.
- **Build the smallest test** — choose the cheapest artifact that can move the signal: a fake-door, a Wizard-of-Oz, a prototype, or a thin MVP slice. The build's job is learning, not shipping the final thing.
- **Measure the signal** — instrument the success metric up front and read it against a pre-committed threshold, so the result can actually falsify the belief (no goalpost-moving after the fact).
- **Decide on evidence** — three honest outcomes: **persevere** (signal hit, scale it), **pivot** (signal missed, change the approach), or **kill** (no signal, free the capacity). Each decision spawns the next hypothesis.
- **Example — Intercom messenger:** rather than build a full live-chat suite, the team hypothesized "in-app messaging will lift activation," shipped a stripped widget to a cohort, and watched activation before investing in the polished product.
- **Example — feature flags at scale:** teams pairing HDD with continuous delivery (per O'Reilly) release a change behind a flag to 5% of traffic, read the conversion signal in hours, and roll forward or back as evidence dictates.
- **Example — Lean UX design sprint:** a design team assumes a simplified checkout reduces drop-off, expresses it as an HDD statement, runs an A/B on a prototype, and only the validated variant graduates to production.
author: Barry O'Reilly; Jeff Gothelf
when_to_use: When a feature rests on an unproven assumption and you can ship a small test to validate it before committing to full build.
when_not_to_use: When the work is a known, low-uncertainty obligation (compliance, table-stakes parity) where there is nothing to learn.
diagram_anchors: **Hypothesis-Driven Development (HDD)**, popularized by Barry O'Reilly (ThoughtWorks) and Jeff Gothelf (Lean UX), reframes every feature as a falsifiable bet

name: Job Story
summary: Klement & Intercom's requirement format: "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]" — context over personas.
body_md:
- A **Job Story** is a context-first requirement format — popularized by Alan Klement with Paul Adams' team at Intercom — written as "When **[situation]**, I want to **[motivation]**, so I can **[expected outcome]**," replacing the persona-laden user story with a causal trigger.
- **When [situation]** — the circumstance, anxiety, or trigger that puts the user in motion. Forces you to name the moment of struggle instead of a demographic ("As a busy manager...").
- **I want to [motivation]** — what the user is trying to do in that moment, stated as a goal, not a UI solution. Keeps the design space open.
- **So I can [expected outcome]** — the result that defines success and lets you test whether any solution actually did the job.
- **Why it differs from the user story** — the classic "As a [persona], I want [X], so that [Y]" smuggles in an assumed user and often an assumed solution. The job story drops the persona, leads with situation, and treats causality (what causes the need) as the design input — drawing directly on Jobs-to-be-Done thinking.
- **Example — Intercom inbox:** "When an important customer messages us, I want to be alerted immediately, so I can respond before they churn." The trigger and stakes shape the solution (real-time alerts), not a persona.
- **Example — Pocket / read-later:** "When I find a long article I have no time to read, I want to save it in one tap, so I can read it later without losing the link." No mention of who the user is — the situation does the work.
- **Example — ride-hailing:** "When I land in an unfamiliar city at night, I want a ride I can trust without haggling, so I can get to my hotel safely." Anchors design on context (unfamiliar, night, trust) over a rider persona.
author: Alan Klement
when_to_use: When you want a requirement that captures the triggering context and causal motivation behind a feature rather than an assumed user type.
when_not_to_use: When the work is a known technical task with no behavioral context to model, where a plain task or acceptance criterion is lighter.
diagram_anchors: A **Job Story** is a context-first requirement format

name: KJ Method
summary: Kawakita Jiro's bottom-up synthesis: cluster raw observations by intrinsic affinity, title the groups, and narrate emergent themes.
body_md:
- The **KJ Method** (named for its originator **K**awakita **J**iro, also called **affinity diagramming**) is a bottom-up synthesis technique that turns a pile of individual qualitative observations into emergent themes by grouping them on intrinsic similarity rather than into predefined buckets.
- **Write observations on cards** — one discrete fact, quote, or note per card (sticky or index card). Keep them concrete and verbatim; do not pre-summarize or pre-categorize.
- **Group by intrinsic affinity** — spread all cards out, then silently move cards that "feel like they belong together" into clusters. The categories are *discovered* from the data, not imposed from a template; orphan cards are allowed to stand alone.
- **Title the clusters** — write a short header card for each group that captures the *essence* of why those cards cohere. A good title is a claim, not a label ("Users distrust the price until they see a peer using it"), so it carries meaning on its own.
- **Narrate the emergent themes** — arrange the titled clusters spatially, draw relationships (causes, tensions, sequences) between them, and tell the story that the whole wall implies — the insight no single card stated.
- Examples: a UX team dumps 200 interview quotes onto a wall, clusters them, and surfaces "onboarding confusion" and "pricing trust" as themes that no script had asked about. A support team affinity-maps a quarter of tickets and discovers a single root cause spanning three "different" complaint types. A strategy offsite uses KJ to converge a brainstorm of sticky notes into three contending bets.
author: Jiro Kawakita
when_to_use: Synthesizing messy qualitative data (interviews, field notes, support tickets) into themes without forcing them into a predefined template.
when_not_to_use: When you already have a fixed coding scheme or just need to fill a known template — the open clustering is wasted overhead.
diagram_anchors: bottom-up synthesis technique that turns a pile of individual qualitative observations into emergent themes

name: Pretotyping
summary: Savoia's pre-build demand test: fake a product's core experience as cheaply as possible to prove people want it before you build it.
body_md:
- **Pretotyping**, coined by Alberto Savoia (ex-Google engineering director), tests whether you're building **"the Right It"** — a product people will actually use — by faking its core experience as cheaply as possible *before* investing in building "It right."
- **The Right It vs. building It right** — Savoia's premise is that most new products fail in the market not from poor execution but because they were the *wrong* it; a pretotype answers the demand question first, a prototype answers the buildability question later.
- **Pick a faking technique** to simulate the experience without the engineering:
	- **Mechanical Turk** — replace the expensive machine/algorithm with hidden humans (e.g. IBM's 1950s speech demo, modern "AI" run by people).
	- **Fake Door** — advertise a product/feature that doesn't exist yet and count who tries to walk through the door.
	- **Pinocchio** — build a lifeless, non-functional mock-up to test whether you'd even use the real thing.
	- **Façade / YouTube / One-Night Stand** — a thin storefront, a demo video, or a single pop-up event that stand in for a finished product.
- **Collect Your Own Data (YODA)** — pretotyping is a data discipline, not just a hack: gather *your own* numbers from people with skin in the game (clicks, sign-ups, pre-orders, cash), never opinions or "would you use this?" surveys.
- **Quantify with a market-engagement hypothesis (the XYZ form)** — state it as "at least **X%** of **Y** will **Z**," run the pretotype, and compare results to that threshold to decide build / pivot / kill.
- **Worked examples:**
	- **Palm Pilot** — Jeff Hawkins carved a block of wood the size of the device and "used" it for weeks (pretending to take notes, check calendar) to test whether a pocket organiser fit his life — the original Pinocchio.
	- **IBM speech-to-text** — instead of building expensive voice recognition, IBM hid a fast typist behind the screen (Mechanical Turk) and found users wanted the *experience* but not the noisy office of dictation.
	- **Zappos** — Nick Swinmurn photographed shoes in local stores and listed them online, buying retail to fulfil any order — a Façade pretotype proving people would buy shoes unseen before building inventory or logistics.
author: Alberto Savoia
when_to_use: Before committing real engineering to a new product or feature, when the open question is "does anyone actually want this?"
when_not_to_use: When demand is already proven and the real risk is technical feasibility or quality of execution.
diagram_anchors: **Pretotyping**, coined by Alberto Savoia (ex-Google engineering director), tests whether you're building **"the Right It"**

name: Riskiest Assumption Test
summary: Rik Higham's de-risking move: isolate the single assumption most likely to kill the idea and run the cheapest test for just that.
body_md:
- **Riskiest Assumption Test (RAT)** — popularized by Rik Higham (Skyscanner) in his 2016 essay "The MVP is dead. Long live the RAT"; instead of building a minimum product, you identify the single assumption most likely to kill your idea and design the cheapest possible test for just that one belief.
- **The critique it answers:** "MVP" has drifted to mean a shoddy first release — too complex to be a quick test, too flimsy to be a real product. RAT refocuses on the original goal: maximize learning per unit of effort, not ship a tiny product.
- **Step 1 — List the assumptions.** Surface every belief the idea depends on: that the problem exists, that users want this solution, that they'll pay, that you can build it, that it's reachable.
- **Step 2 — Rank by risk.** Risk = likelihood the assumption is wrong × damage if it is. The riskiest is the one whose failure invalidates everything downstream.
- **Step 3 — Design the cheapest test for that one assumption.** A RAT is *not* a product — it's a fake door, a landing page, a concierge or Wizard-of-Oz run, interviews, or a clickable prototype. Minimize cost-to-evidence.
- **Step 4 — Learn, then loop.** Validated → move to the next-riskiest assumption and build confidence incrementally. Invalidated → pivot or kill before you've sunk real money.
- **Example — marketplace idea.** The riskiest assumption is usually demand, not supply or tech. A fake-door landing page measuring sign-up intent tests it in days, before any inventory or code exists.
- **Example — Dropbox.** The riskiest assumption was "people want frictionless sync enough to care," not "we can build it." Drew Houston's explainer video (a RAT, not a product) drove the beta waitlist from 5k to 75k overnight.
- **Example — Zappos.** Nick Swinmurn's riskiest assumption was "people will buy shoes online unseen." He photographed shoes in local stores and bought them at retail to fulfill orders — testing demand without inventory or a warehouse.
author: Rik Higham
when_to_use: Early in discovery when an idea rests on one shaky belief that, if false, makes everything else moot.
when_not_to_use: When the core assumptions are already validated and the real work is engineering a shippable, scalable product.
diagram_anchors: **Riskiest Assumption Test (RAT)** — popularized by Rik Higham (Skyscanner) in his 2016 essay

name: Switch Interview
summary: Bob Moesta's timeline interview: replay a real purchase and weigh Four Forces — Push + Pull vs. Anxiety + Habit — to surface the true job.
body_md:
- The **Switch Interview**, developed by Bob Moesta and Chris Spiek (and central to Jobs-to-be-Done practice), is a retrospective, timeline-based customer interview that reconstructs one real purchase moment-by-moment to reveal the "job" the product was hired to do.
- **Build the timeline.** Walk backward through the actual decision — **first thought**, **passive looking**, **active looking**, the **switch**, and **first use** — pinning each step to dates, places, and people, using interrogation-style recall ("what day was it?", "where were you sitting?") to defeat vague memory.
- **The Four Forces** that govern every switch, two driving change and two resisting it:
	- **Push** — the pain or frustration of the current situation that makes the status quo untenable.
	- **Pull** — the magnetic attraction of the new solution and the better life it promises.
	- **Anxiety** — fear and uncertainty about the new choice (will it work? what will I lose?).
	- **Habit** — the comfortable inertia of what the person already owns and uses.
- **The balance rule:** a switch only happens when **Push + Pull > Anxiety + Habit**; the interview's goal is to locate the *struggling moment* where that balance tipped, because that moment is the real job.
- **Design implication:** product teams usually over-invest in Pull (more features) while ignoring Anxiety and Habit — the forces that quietly kill adoption; the interview tells you which force to reduce.
- **Example — mattresses (Moesta's classic):** interviews revealed people didn't switch for "better sleep" but at a struggling moment like moving in with a partner or a back injury; the job was a life transition, not a comfort spec.
- **Example — Snickers vs. Milky Way:** the same candy bar is hired for different jobs — Snickers to *fill me up and keep me going* (push: hunger), Milky Way to *treat myself* — so they compete with different products and need different positioning.
- **Example — software switch:** a team migrating from a legacy tool reveals that Anxiety (data migration risk) and Habit (muscle memory) outweighed a clearly superior new tool, telling the vendor to invest in onboarding and import, not more capability.
author: Bob Moesta
when_to_use: Reconstructing why a real customer actually switched to (or away from) your product to uncover the job they hired it for.
when_not_to_use: When no real purchase has happened yet — the method depends on a concrete past switch, so for pre-product idea validation use a problem interview instead.
diagram_anchors: The **Switch Interview**, developed by Bob Moesta and Chris Spiek (and central to Jobs-to-be-Done practice), is a retrospective, timeline-based customer interview

name: The Mom Test
summary: Rob Fitzpatrick's interview discipline: validate ideas by asking about real past behavior, not opinions, dodging compliments, fluff, and hypotheticals.
body_md:
- The **Mom Test**, named by Rob Fitzpatrick (2013), is a set of question-design rules for customer interviews that get truthful data even from people who want to be nice to you — the premise being that even your mom will lie to you if you ask whether your idea is good.
