- **Rule 1 — Talk about their life, not your idea.** Keep your idea out of the room. People can't reliably evaluate a pitch, but they can describe how they actually do things today.
- **Rule 2 — Ask about specifics in the past, not opinions about the future.** "What would you do?" invites flattering fiction; "Walk me through the last time this happened" surfaces real workflows, workarounds, and money spent.
- **Rule 3 — Talk less, listen more.** Your job is to learn, not to sell; the more you pitch, the more you bias the answer.
- **The three bad-data traps** to recognize and discard:
	- **Compliments** — "That's a great idea!" is social politeness, not validation; ignore it and dig for facts.
	- **Fluff** — generic, hypothetical, future-tense talk ("I usually...", "I would..."); anchor back to a concrete past instance.
	- **Hypotheticals** — "I'd definitely buy that" is a promise about an imagined future; chase actual past behavior and real budget instead.
- **Good signals** are *commitments and advances* — the person gives up something they value (time, reputation, money): a follow-up meeting, an intro, a pre-order, access to data.
- **Example — the failed pitch:** asking "Would you buy an app that helps you cook healthy?" gets warm yeses; the Mom-Test version — "Tell me about the last time you tried to eat better. What happened? What did you spend on it?" — reveals whether the problem is real and funded.
- **Example — Justin Wilcox / dropbox-style discovery:** founders who replaced "Do you like this?" with "When did you last lose a file, and what did it cost you?" stopped collecting compliments and started hearing the actual pain (or its absence) before writing code.
author: Rob Fitzpatrick
when_to_use: Running early customer-discovery conversations to validate a problem or idea before building.
when_not_to_use: When you already have a shipped product and want quantitative signal — instrument behavior and run experiments instead of interviews.
diagram_anchors: The **Mom Test**, named by Rob Fitzpatrick (2013), is a set of question-design rules for customer interviews

name: Wizard of Oz MVP
summary: Kelley's behind-the-curtain test: users hit a finished-looking product while a human secretly does the "automated" work, validating demand before you build.
body_md:
- The **Wizard of Oz MVP**, named after the man behind the curtain (term coined by researcher Jeff Kelley in 1983), is a test where users interact with what looks like a fully automated product while a **human invisibly performs the work behind the scenes** — letting you measure real demand for a capability you haven't built yet.
- **Build a believable front stage** — a polished UI, email, or chat that behaves exactly as the finished product would; the user must never suspect a person is involved (this covertness is what distinguishes it from the overt **Concierge MVP**, where users know the service is manual).
- **Put a human behind the curtain** — instead of the algorithm/integration/automation, a teammate manually types the answer, picks the result, or fulfils the order in real time, so the experience feels instant and automated.
- **Instrument for desirability** — because the experience is real, you get honest signal: do people complete the flow, come back, pay, recommend? You learn *whether the magic is wanted* before sinking months into making it real.
- **Graduate or kill** — if demand holds, automate the wizard's job piece by piece; if it doesn't, you've spent days, not quarters.
- **Worked example — Zappos:** Nick Swinmurn photographed shoes in local stores and listed them online with no inventory; when an order came in he bought the pair at retail and shipped it — proving people would buy shoes online before building a warehouse or supply chain.
- **Worked example — Aardvark (social Q&A, acquired by Google):** the team faked the smart routing engine with humans relaying questions to likely experts over IM, validating that people valued fast human-curated answers before building the matching algorithm.
- **Worked example — an "AI" scheduling assistant:** users email the bot to book meetings; early on humans read the threads and schedule manually behind the polished agent persona, confirming people trust and adopt the flow before the NLP is built.
author: Jeff Kelley (term, 1983)
when_to_use: When you need to prove people will actually use a not-yet-built capability before paying to engineer the automation behind it.
when_not_to_use: When the open question is feasibility or scale, not desirability — faking the engine tells you nothing about whether you can really build it.
diagram_anchors: users interact with what looks like a fully automated product while a **human invisibly performs the work behind the scenes**

name: Arc Product-Market Fit Framework
summary: Sequoia's three PMF archetypes: classify the kind of fit you're chasing — Hair on Fire, Hard Fact, or Future Vision — each needing its own GTM.
body_md:
- **Arc Product-Market Fit Framework** — popularized by Sequoia Capital (2023) — argues there isn't one shape of product-market fit but three distinct archetypes, and your go-to-market, competitive posture, and risk profile must be tailored to whichever one you're in.
- **Hair on Fire** — an urgent, widely-felt problem in a crowded market. Customers are already shopping for a fix, so the market is saturated and competitive; you win by being clearly **best-in-class** on execution and speed. The hard part is differentiation, not demand.
- **Hard Fact** — a chronic pain that customers have accepted as the status quo in a stagnant market. Nobody is actively looking, so your job is to make the problem feel worth solving and offer an approach **novel enough** to overcome inertia and "good enough." The hard part is breaking resignation.
- **Future Vision** — reimagining the world: solving a problem people don't yet feel, in a market that doesn't exist. Demand must be **created and educated**; this path has exponentially more ways to fail but the biggest payoff. The hard part is conviction and timing.
- **Examples** —
	- **Zoom (Hair on Fire):** video conferencing was a known, painful, crowded category; Zoom won by being dramatically more reliable and easier — best-in-class execution, not a new problem.
	- **Notion (Hard Fact):** people had tolerated scattered docs, wikis, and task apps for years; Notion's novel all-in-one block model made a long-accepted mess suddenly worth fixing.
	- **Apple / iPhone (Future Vision):** Apple's 1978 memo to Sequoia admitted near-zero demand for home computers. The iPhone launched as a Future Vision in 2007 — then the smartphone category quickly hardened into Hair on Fire dynamics, showing archetypes can shift over time.
author: Sequoia Capital
when_to_use: When you've found early demand and need to name what kind of PMF you have so your GTM, messaging, and risk plan match it.
when_not_to_use: When you have no signal of demand yet — it classifies a fit you already sense, it doesn't help you discover one.
diagram_anchors: **Arc Product-Market Fit Framework** — popularized by Sequoia Capital (2023) — argues there isn't one shape of product-market fit but three distinct archetypes

name: Lean Product Process
summary: Dan Olsen's PMF pyramid + 6-step loop: stack target customer, needs, value, features and UX, then iterate to test fit.
body_md:
- The **Lean Product Process**, defined by Dan Olsen in *The Lean Product Playbook* (2015), is a five-layer **Product-Market Fit Pyramid** paired with a 6-step iterative loop that articulates PMF hypotheses bottom-up and tests them top-down.
- The pyramid's two bottom layers describe the **market** and the top three describe the **product**; product-market fit is how well the product (top) satisfies the market (bottom).
- **Layer 1 — Target Customer** (base): the specific customer segment you are building for; everything above is defined relative to them.
- **Layer 2 — Underserved Needs:** the customer needs that existing solutions meet poorly — the opportunity space, framed as importance vs. current satisfaction.
- **Layer 3 — Value Proposition:** which underserved needs you will meet better than alternatives; your deliberate bet on where to win.
- **Layer 4 — Feature Set:** the specific functionality that delivers the value proposition — the MVP scope.
- **Layer 5 — UX** (top): how features are made tangible to the user; the layer customers actually touch and judge.
- The **6-step loop** operationalizes the climb: (1) determine your target customer, (2) identify underserved needs, (3) define your value proposition, (4) specify your MVP feature set, (5) create your MVP prototype, (6) test the MVP with customers — then iterate.
- Each layer must **fit the layer below it**; a great UX on a feature set that misses the real underserved need still fails. You test the top layer with users, but a failure can force a rethink of any layer beneath.
- **Example — Superhuman:** Rahul Vohra narrowed the target customer to people who felt the product would be "very disappointing" to lose, sharpening every layer above; the climb up the pyramid is exactly this kind of segment-then-need-then-value sequencing.
- **Example — early Dropbox:** the explainer-video MVP tested the value proposition ("frictionless sync") against the target customer's underserved need before the full feature set and polished UX were built — a test-the-top-against-the-base move.
author: Dan Olsen
when_to_use: Pre-PMF, when you need to make each layer of your product strategy explicit and falsifiable before building.
when_not_to_use: After PMF is established and the job shifts to scaling, growth loops, or optimization rather than fit-finding.
diagram_anchors: The **Lean Product Process**, defined by Dan Olsen in *The Lean Product Playbook* (2015), is a five-layer **Product-Market Fit Pyramid** paired with a 6-step iterative loop

name: Levels of Product-Market Fit
summary: Todd Jackson's PMF maturity ladder: reframes product-market fit as four progressive levels — Nascent, Developing, Strong, Extreme.
body_md:
- **Levels of Product-Market Fit** is Todd Jackson's (First Round Capital) reframing of PMF as a progressive ladder rather than a yes/no switch — most B2B startups climb through four levels over 4–6 years, and the team's job plus the signals to watch change at each rung. PMF is graded on three dimensions: **demand**, **satisfaction**, and **efficiency**.
- **Level 1 — Nascent PMF:** A handful of design partners (often 3–5) confirm the problem is real and your solution is plausible. Every conversion takes founder-level effort; you're hand-selling and validating, not scaling.
- **Level 2 — Developing PMF:** Sales become repeatable and somewhat predictable, but demand is still *pushed* — you create it through outbound and heavy lifting rather than the market pulling. Jackson notes ~60% of startups never get past this level.
- **Level 3 — Strong PMF:** Demand begins to *pull* — inbound shows up, retention holds, and scaling starts to feel efficient. The job shifts from proving the product to scaling go-to-market without breaking unit economics.
- **Level 4 — Extreme PMF:** Demand is overwhelming, retention and word-of-mouth are self-reinforcing, and growth is efficient and durable. The job becomes capturing the market and defending it.
- **The 4 Ps lever:** When stuck at a level, Jackson prescribes adjusting one of **Persona, Problem, Promise, or Product** to unlock the next rung, rather than just doing more of the same.
- **Examples:** *Superhuman* sat at Developing PMF — high-touch onboarding, manual demand — and used the Sean Ellis "very disappointed" survey to engineer its way toward Strong. *Ramp* rocketed from Developing to Strong/Extreme as spend-management demand pulled inbound. Early *Dropbox* (Jackson's own past) moved from Nascent design-partner validation to Extreme as referral-driven demand became overwhelming.
author: Todd Jackson
when_to_use: When you have some traction and need to diagnose which level of PMF you're at and what the next level demands.
when_not_to_use: Pre-launch with zero customers, where there's no demand signal yet to grade against the ladder.
diagram_anchors: **Levels of Product-Market Fit** is Todd Jackson's (First Round Capital) reframing of PMF as a progressive ladder rather than a yes/no switch

name: GO Product Roadmap
summary: Roman Pichler's goal-oriented roadmap: a date · name · goal · features · metrics grid that leads each release with the outcome, not a feature list.
body_md:
- **GO Product Roadmap** — Roman Pichler's goal-oriented roadmap template that organizes a product plan around the **goal** each release should achieve, captured in five columns: **date, name, goal, features, metrics**.
- **Date** — the rough timeframe the release ships (a quarter or month, not a precise day), keeping the plan honest about its uncertainty.
- **Name** — a memorable handle for the release so teams and stakeholders can refer to it (e.g. "Onboarding Revamp").
- **Goal** — the organizing unit: the outcome or benefit you intend to create, such as acquire users, increase engagement, or reduce churn. Each release earns its place by serving a goal.
- **Features** — the few high-level capabilities needed to meet the goal — deliberately coarse, so the roadmap does not harden into a feature contract.
- **Metrics** — the measure that proves the goal was met (e.g. activation rate, retention, NPS), tying every release to evidence rather than output.
- Pichler's discipline: pick **one goal per release**, list only the features that goal requires, and never add a feature without a goal it serves — this shifts the conversation from "what will we build" to "what value will we create."
- **Example — a habit app:** Q3 / "Launch" / goal *acquire new users* / features signup + onboarding / metric 10k signups. Q4 / "Hook" / goal *activate faster* / features templates + tips / metric 40% day-1 activation. Q1 / "Stay" / goal *retain cohorts* / features reminders + digests / metric +15% week-4 retention.
- **Example — Slack-style tool:** a single release whose goal is *reduce time-to-first-message*; features = guided workspace setup and sample channels; metric = median minutes to first message, replacing a sprawling list of unranked features.
author: Roman Pichler
when_to_use: When you want a roadmap that aligns stakeholders on outcomes and measurable benefits rather than a committed feature list.
when_not_to_use: When you need detailed delivery scheduling or fine-grained sprint planning — GO is too coarse for that.
diagram_anchors: Roman Pichler's goal-oriented roadmap template that organizes a product plan around the **goal** each release should achieve

name: Now-Next-Later Roadmap
summary: Janna Bastow's three-horizon roadmap: drops dates for Now/Next/Later buckets where detail and commitment shrink as certainty falls.
body_md:
- A roadmap format popularized by Janna Bastow (co-founder of ProdPad) in 2012 that replaces a dated timeline with three confidence horizons — **Now**, **Next**, and **Later** — where the level of detail and commitment decreases as certainty decreases.
- **Now** — what the team is committed to and actively working on; high detail, scoped solutions, discovery largely complete. These are real promises.
- **Next** — what is on deck once Now ships; medium detail, the problem is framed and being validated but solutions are not yet locked. Order is intent, not a queue contract.
- **Later** — directional bets and themes the team believes matter eventually; low detail, expressed as problems/outcomes rather than features. Deliberately fuzzy so options stay open.
- **The core principle** — the further out something is, the more uncertain it is, so the roadmap should show *less* precision the further right you go. Adding fake dates to uncertain work creates false promises the team can't keep.
- Columns are **outcome- and problem-oriented**, not a feature-and-date list; cards move leftward (Later → Next → Now) as evidence accumulates, and can drop out entirely if data says they shouldn't exist.
- Example — **ProdPad** itself ships its public roadmap in this format: "Now" lists active integrations, "Next" lists validated problem areas, "Later" lists broad themes like "deeper analytics."
- Example — many teams render it as three swim-lanes per product area in tools like Trello, Jira, or ProdPad, reviewed each sprint/cadence rather than re-baselined quarterly.
- Contrast — unlike a date-bucketed quarterly roadmap (this-quarter / next-quarter), Now-Next-Later attaches commitment to *confidence*, not the calendar, so a slip doesn't read as a missed deadline.
author: Janna Bastow
when_to_use: When you need a flexible, outcome-oriented roadmap that survives changing data without breaking deadline promises.
when_not_to_use: When stakeholders genuinely require committed delivery dates (e.g. a contractual launch, a regulatory deadline, a coordinated GTM event).
diagram_anchors: A roadmap format popularized by Janna Bastow (co-founder of ProdPad) in 2012 that replaces a dated timeline with three confidence horizons

name: Influence Without Authority
summary: Cohen & Bradford's currency-exchange model: get cooperation from people you don't manage by trading what they value, grounded in reciprocity.
body_md:
- **Influence Without Authority** is a model from Allan Cohen and David Bradford: because organizations run on interdependence and you rarely control the people you depend on, you earn cooperation by treating it as an exchange — you offer something the other person values (a "currency") in return for the help you need, governed by the norm of reciprocity.
- **Assume the other person is a potential ally, not an adversary** — frame the interaction as mutual exchange between people who each have legitimate goals and pressures, not a battle to win.
- **Know their world** — figure out the forces acting on them: their objectives, what their boss measures them on, their constraints. You can't pay a currency you haven't identified.
- **Know your own goals and the currencies you control** — be clear what you actually need (separate it from your ego and tactics) and inventory what you can offer: budget, headcount, a fast review, public credit, an introduction, a returned favor.
- **The five currency families** — **Inspiration** (vision, excellence, a moral cause); **Task** (resources, information, technical help, organizational support); **Position** (recognition, visibility, reputation, important contacts); **Relationship** (acceptance, belonging, personal support); **Personal** (gratitude, ownership, self-concept, comfort). Match the currency to what *they* value, not what you'd value.
- **Make the exchange, and tend the relationship** — trade in a way that fits how you relate; pay currencies before you need to draw on them so the "bank account" of goodwill is already full when you ask. Over-drafting — taking without giving — closes the account.
- **Worked example (PM ↔ Eng lead):** You need a platform team to prioritize an API your roadmap depends on, but you don't manage them. Their currency is *Position* and *Inspiration*: their lead wants the team seen as strategic. You offer visibility — co-present the work to leadership, credit them explicitly, and frame it as the foundation for a company bet. Cooperation follows because you paid a currency they value.
- **Worked example (PM ↔ Legal):** Legal keeps deprioritizing your review. Their currency is *Task* and *Personal* — predictability and reduced last-minute fire-drills. You offer a standing two-week lead time and a clean, pre-structured brief; in exchange they fast-track you. You traded their pain (chaos) for your need (speed).
author: Allan Cohen & David Bradford
when_to_use: When you need real cooperation from a cross-functional peer or senior you can't direct, and asking nicely isn't moving them.
when_not_to_use: When you do hold authority and the request is non-negotiable, or when the relationship needs trust built first and any "trade" would read as transactional.
diagram_anchors: **Influence Without Authority** is a model from Allan Cohen and David Bradford

name: Stakeholder Salience Model
summary: Mitchell, Agle & Wood's three-circle model: ranks stakeholders by power, legitimacy and urgency into seven priority classes.
body_md:
- A stakeholder-prioritization model, introduced by Mitchell, Agle & Wood (1997), that classifies stakeholders by how many of three attributes they hold — **power**, **legitimacy** and **urgency** — yielding seven classes whose salience rises with each attribute added.
- **Power** — the stakeholder's ability to impose its will on the firm (budget, headcount, veto, regulatory force).
- **Legitimacy** — whether the stakeholder's claim is seen as proper or appropriate within shared norms (a paying customer, a regulator, an employee).
- **Urgency** — the degree to which the claim demands immediate attention (time-sensitive and important to the stakeholder).
- **Latent stakeholders (one attribute)** — low salience, watch but don't over-invest:
	- **Dormant** — power only (a once-loud investor now quiet).
	- **Discretionary** — legitimacy only (a community group with no leverage).
	- **Demanding** — urgency only (the persistent complainer with no real claim or clout).
- **Expectant stakeholders (two attributes)** — moderate salience, actively manage:
	- **Dominant** — power + legitimacy (your exec sponsor, key enterprise account).
	- **Dependent** — legitimacy + urgency, but no power, so they rely on others to act (users hit by an outage who need an advocate).
	- **Dangerous** — power + urgency without legitimacy (a coercive actor who can force action through illegitimate means).
- **Definitive stakeholders (all three)** — highest salience, prioritize now (a major regulator issuing a deadline; a strategic customer threatening churn over a live SLA breach).
- Salience is **dynamic**: a Dependent group that gains a powerful champion becomes Definitive overnight, so the map is re-read each cycle, not set once.
- **Example** — A PM facing a GDPR deadline treats the data-protection authority as **Definitive** (power + legitimacy + urgency) and a vocal-but-niche feature-request forum as merely **Demanding** (urgency only), allocating roadmap and comms accordingly.
author: Mitchell, Agle & Wood
when_to_use: When you must decide which stakeholders genuinely warrant attention and in what order, beyond a flat power/interest grid.
when_not_to_use: For day-to-day RACI role assignment or when the stakeholder set is small and stable enough that a simple list suffices.
diagram_anchors: A stakeholder-prioritization model, introduced by Mitchell, Agle & Wood (1997), that classifies stakeholders by how many of three attributes they hold

name: Trust Equation
summary: Maister, Green & Galford's formula: trust grows with credibility, reliability and intimacy, and collapses as self-interest shows.
body_md:
- The **Trust Equation**, from Maister, Green & Galford's *The Trusted Advisor* (2000), models interpersonal trustworthiness as a computable fraction: **Trust = (Credibility + Reliability + Intimacy) ÷ Self-Orientation** — three numerators build trust, one denominator destroys it.
- **Credibility** — the words you speak; "can I be believed?" Built by expertise, accurate claims, and not bluffing. *Example:* a PM who says "I don't know, but I'll find out by Friday" instead of faking an answer.
- **Reliability** — your actions over time; "do you do what you say?" Built by keeping small promises consistently, not just the big ones. *Example:* always sending the follow-up doc you promised in the meeting.
- **Intimacy** — emotional safety; "can people be vulnerable with me?" The most underused lever — built by listening, discretion, and admitting your own gaps. *Example:* an engineering lead confiding a slipping estimate early because they know you won't punish honesty.
- **Self-Orientation** — the denominator; "are you in this for yourself or for me?" Self-serving behaviour (talking over people, name-dropping, rushing to your own agenda) inflates it and tanks the whole equation, no matter how strong the numerators.
- The math is the lesson: because self-orientation divides, a single visible act of self-interest can wipe out hard-won credibility and reliability. The fastest path to trust is usually *lowering the denominator*, not adding credentials.
- *Worked example:* A vendor PM with deep expertise (high credibility) and on-time delivery (high reliability) still loses the renewal because every call steers toward upsell (high self-orientation). A rival with less polish but genuine focus on the customer's roadmap wins — lower denominator, higher trust.
author: David Maister, Charles Green & Robert Galford
when_to_use: When you need to diagnose why a stakeholder, partner or customer does not yet trust you and decide which lever to pull.
when_not_to_use: When the gap is structural (misaligned incentives, no authority) rather than interpersonal — no amount of trust-building fixes a broken org design.
diagram_anchors: models interpersonal trustworthiness as a computable fraction: **Trust = (Credibility + Reliability + Intimacy) ÷ Self-Orientation**

name: LLM Evals - Three Gulfs
summary: Shankar & Husain's failure-surface map: names the three gulfs in an LLM pipeline and the Analyze-Measure-Improve loop that closes them.
body_md:
- The **Three Gulfs** model, from Shreya Shankar and Hamel Husain's *AI Evals for Engineers & PMs*, names the three places an LLM pipeline breaks down — between the developer, the data, and the model — and pairs them with an **Analyze → Measure → Improve** lifecycle for systematically closing them.
- **Gulf of Comprehension** (developer ↔ data): you cannot read every input your system sees, so you don't actually know the shape of the data, the edge cases, or how often each failure mode occurs. Closed by reading samples and open-coding traces.
- **Gulf of Specification** (developer ↔ LLM): natural-language prompts can't fully capture your intent — what's obvious to you is unstated to the model, so it guesses. Closed by tightening prompts, adding examples, and decomposing tasks.
- **Gulf of Generalization** (LLM ↔ data): even a well-specified prompt that works on the cases you tried won't necessarily hold on unseen inputs. Closed by measuring on held-out data and hardening the pipeline (retrieval, decomposition, guardrails).
- The **lifecycle** that bridges all three:
	- **Analyze** — collect real traces, open-code them, cluster failures into a taxonomy (this attacks Comprehension).
	- **Measure** — turn the top failure modes into evals: code-based assertions or LLM-as-judge graders, validated against human labels.
	- **Improve** — change prompts, pipeline, or model; re-measure; repeat until the failure rate drops.
- **Worked example — a customer-support RAG bot.** *Analyze:* sampling 100 transcripts reveals it hallucinates refund policy on multi-product orders (Comprehension). *Measure:* an LLM judge scores "policy-grounded?" on a labeled set, confirming a 22% failure rate. *Improve:* you decompose the prompt and add a retrieval step for policy docs (Specification), then re-measure on fresh tickets to confirm it holds (Generalization).
- **Worked example — an invoice-extraction pipeline.** Demos looked perfect, but Analyze on real PDFs surfaced multi-currency and rotated-scan failures; a measured eval quantified them, and per-field decomposition plus a validation grader closed the gap.
author: Shreya Shankar & Hamel Husain
when_to_use: When an LLM feature works in demos but fails unpredictably and you need a structured way to find, measure, and fix the failures.
when_not_to_use: For deterministic software with stable specs, where conventional unit tests and a clear pass/fail are enough.
diagram_anchors: The **Three Gulfs** model, from Shreya Shankar and Hamel Husain's *AI Evals for Engineers & PMs*, names the three places an LLM pipeline breaks down

name: People + AI Guidebook
summary: Google PAIR's design-pattern library: six chapters of human-centered patterns for building trustworthy AI products.
body_md:
- The **People + AI Guidebook** is Google PAIR's open design-pattern library (2019; updated for generative AI in 2023) that organizes human-centered AI product decisions into six chapters, each with worksheets and worked patterns.
- **User Needs + Defining Success** — decide whether AI is even the right tool, and define what a good outcome looks like (the "reward function") before building. Augment vs. automate.
- **Data Collection + Evaluation** — source, label, and continuously evaluate training data responsibly, since the data shapes every downstream behavior the user feels.
- **Mental Models** — set accurate user expectations of what the system can and can't do, onboard for capability and limitation, and avoid over-promising.
- **Explainability + Trust** — decide how, when, and how much to explain model output so users calibrate trust rather than blindly accepting or rejecting it.
- **Feedback + Control** — give users levers to correct, steer, opt out of, and teach the system, turning a black box into a collaborator.
- **Errors + Graceful Failure** — anticipate model errors (false positives, low confidence, off-distribution input) and design recovery paths so failure degrades gracefully instead of breaking trust.
- **Generative-AI update** — adds patterns for prompting, managing hallucination, citing sources, and shaping expectations of open-ended LLM output across all six chapters.
- **Example — Gmail Smart Compose:** sets a clear mental model (suggestions, not auto-send), gives instant control (Tab to accept, ignore to dismiss), and fails gracefully by simply showing nothing when unsure.
- **Example — Google Photos search:** explains results loosely ("things that look like…"), and lets users correct mislabeled faces — a feedback loop that improves the model and signals fallibility.
- **Example — generative assistants (Bard/Gemini):** apply the gen-AI patterns — visible "draft" framing, regenerate/edit controls, and source citations to manage hallucination and trust.
author: Google People + AI Research (PAIR)
when_to_use: When you are designing the UX of an AI/ML or generative-AI feature and need patterns for trust, control, and failure.
when_not_to_use: When the problem is non-AI or the bottleneck is model accuracy rather than the human-facing experience.
diagram_anchors: The **People + AI Guidebook** is Google PAIR's open design-pattern library (2019; updated for generative AI in 2023) that organizes human-centered AI product decisions into six chapters
